data.title: EWZ: Valuation Is Reasonable, But Business Quality Remains A Concern
data.description:
The S&P 500 and NASDAQ are rising from one all-time high to the next, and valuation levels are reaching historical extremes. Therefore, it might be wise to diversify globally going forward.

Emerging markets have disappointed over the last decade. This has led to attractive valuations, which could lead to outperformance over the next decade. One of the biggest mistakes I see the majority of investors commit is extrapolating past performance into the future.

According to this source, Brazil is one of the most attractively valued markets globally. Today, we're going to dive deeper into the iShares MSCI Brazil ETF (NYSEARCA:EWZ) to see if it offers value in an exuberant market environment.

iShares MSCI Brazil ETF

EWZ seeks exposure to large and mid-sized companies in Brazil. The net expense ratio is 0.59%, which is on the high side for an ETF, but in line with peers that provide a single-country view.

The turnover rate is 22%, which seems a little too high. It leads to unnecessary transaction fees that drag on performance. Combined with the relatively high expense ratio, and a decent amount of performance is eaten up by fees.

P/E Ratio

One of the strongest selling points for Brazilian equities is the relatively low valuations. Yahoo Finance claims EWZ's average P/E ratio is 10.62, while the Wall Street Journal (WSJ) puts it at 13.91.

Having analyzed dozens of ETFs already, the WSJ numbers seem to be more accurate for the earnings multiple on a TTM basis. But to make sure, we're going to calculate the average P/E for the top holdings a little later on.

In both cases, the valuations look relatively attractive, as long as the underlying businesses are of quality, which we're going to find out down below.

Dividend Yield

A low valuation usually leads to high dividend yields, and EWZ is no exception. In 2024, EWZ distributed $2.01, and the current share price is $31.32 for a dividend yield of 6.4%.

In the first half of 2025, however, the distributions were cut by 52.82%, which will likely lead to a lower forward dividend yield. If we assume EWZ will distribute $1.60 this year, the yield drops to 5.11%.

A 5% to 6% dividend yield looks very attractive in the current environment, but just by looking at EWZ distributions over the last decades, it becomes clear that the underlying businesses are cyclical. In other words, we can't rely on the dividends.

Cyclical businesses tend to be poor long-term performers, unless bought at the low points during severe bear markets. EWZ distributions have risen from $0.64 in 2001 (its first full year after inception) to $2.81 in 2010, only to fall back to $0.60 in the 6 years thereafter.

Since then, they have been on a steady rise to an all-time high of $3.52 set in 2022, and over the last few years have declined rapidly. Not surprisingly, the distributions tend to rise and fall with the commodity cycles, as Brazil's economy tends to be commodity-focused.

Sector Allocation

The largest sector is financials, with 37.55% of total assets invested, followed by energy, utilities, basic materials and industrials.

The sector allocation isn't unusual for an emerging market, but it focuses on low-growth and cyclical industries, which likely won't lead to outsized long-term total returns, unless bought at very low multiples.

Top 10 Holdings

EWZ only holds 59 stocks, which isn't unusual for a smaller single country ETF. The top 10 holdings represent 57.10% of total assets. It's pretty concentrated and shouldn't be a core holding in a well-diversified portfolio.

Nu Holdings leads the list with a 12.34% weight, followed by Itau Unibanco, Vale, and Petrobras.

Top Holdings: Valuations, Growth Rates & Quality

When it comes to determining future expected returns, there are three important metrics: the starting valuation, the expected growth rates, and the business quality.

Ideally, we want to buy high-quality companies with high growth rates at rock-bottom valuations. While that sounds too good to be true, if we're patient and turn enough rocks, those kinds of companies can be found. But in this exuberant market environment, where bargains aren't abundant, it leads to a concentrated portfolio.

If we buy a broader ETF (in a world of high valuations), we have to either pay more for our stocks or cut back on our quality or growth expectations. To determine if EWZ offers an attractive risk/reward profile at current valuations, let's dive deeper into the companies. And we're going to start with the average P/E ratio and the past growth rates over the commodity cycles.

Company: P/E Ratio Annual EPS Growth Rate (Past 10 Years):
Nu Holdings 34.36 Nil
Itau Unibanco 8.63 0%
Vale 8.48 Nil
Petrobras 6.18 Nil
Petrobras 6.18 Nil
Bank Bradesco 8.18 Nil
B3 SA 14.90 7.89%
Companhia De Saneamento 9.34 33.42%
Centrais Electricas Brasileiras 18.70 Nil
Weg 30.87 20.26%

(Gurufocus)

Just by looking at the cash flow statements over the last 10 years, it becomes clear that EWZ mostly holds what I perceive to be below-average businesses.

Most of the companies generate erratic cash flows, deeply negative at times. Over the cycles, there's not much growth, if any. It's not ideal for long-term investment.

Investors in EWZ need to hope for a prolonged and sustained commodity price increase to generate outsized returns, which in my view, makes the ETF speculative. Unless it's trading at rock-bottom valuations (and currently it's not), it won't make my personal portfolio.

That said, the average P/E ratio of the largest companies is 14.58, which is roughly in line with the numbers provided by the WSJ. Given the quality of the underlying businesses, the valuation doesn't impress me, but it's low enough to not rate it a "sell" but a "hold".

EWZ Performance Over 25 Years

EWZ has been around for 25 years; that's a long enough time to judge its performance. Over a quarter of a century, the ETF has gained 321% on a total return basis. That's a 5.92% CAGR, dividends included.

If we just look at the share price performance, results have been even less impressive.

The share price gained 66% since inception, which is a 2.05% CAGR and reflects the below-average quality of the underlying businesses and the sluggish growth over the cycles. As long as we don't enter a major commodity bull, an ETF like EWZ will likely keep disappointing.

Comparison Against Other ETFs

Over the last few years, EWZ has outperformed the Global X Brazil Active ETF (BRAZ), but underperformed the Franklin FTSE Brazil ETF (FLBR).

If we judge EWZ's and FLBR's performance over a longer time horizon, however, it's almost identical. Only the VanEck Brazil Small-Cap ETF (BRF) keeps underperforming.

Investing in small-caps, especially in emerging markets, may need an active approach.

Conclusion

On one hand, EWZ trades at a modest valuation, offers a compelling (but unreliable) dividend yield, and provides exposure to an emerging market with growth prospects. On the other hand, the quality of the underlying businesses is questionable, growth has been subdued and choppy at best, and the long-term performance isn't convincing.

As long as we don't see another major commodity bull, EWZ will likely keep underperforming, which makes the ETF more of a speculation than a compelling long-term investment. I rate it a "hold" at current valuations, which aren't excessive, but also not in bargain territory.

I believe other emerging markets like the Philippines offer higher quality at similar valuations, even though the dividend yield is less tempting.

data.title: Albemarle Corporation DEP SHS REPSTG A: Mandatory Converts, Not Traditional Preferred Shares
data.description:
Thesis

Many investors just look at naming conventions and yields and forget to do a full due diligence on what they buy. Retail investors need to understand that in finance a naming convention does not always translate to a familiar reality, and investors need to read the 'fine print'.

In today's article we are going to deal with the Albemarle Corporation DEP SHS REPSTG A (NYSE:ALB.PR.A) and highlight for readers what these securities actually are, what they offer in terms of risks and rewards, and why they were issued in the first place.

Who is Albemarle? A lithium producer

Let us start off with the parent company, namely Albemarle (NYSE:ALB):

Albemarle Corporation is an American specialty chemicals manufacturing company headquartered in Charlotte, North Carolina. The company is a global leader in the production of lithium.

Lithium is a critical component of batteries, including EV batteries. After a massive spike in prices in 2022, lithium pricing has come back to a more palatable historic trend:

As you would expect with any commodity producer, ALB saw its common shares skyrocket with lithium prices, only to come back down to earth in the past two years:

After spiking up more than 240%, the common shares are back to flat on a 5-year lookback. More importantly though, the company has been taking the correct measures to improve its balance sheet after chasing higher lithium prices with capex.

Why did the company issue mandatory convertibles? Balance sheet optimization

Let us start by looking at the capital structure of the company:

As per Seeking Alpha the company has $3.7 billion in total debt and $9.4 billion in market cap. The net debt figure is lower because ALB has a very large cash balance.

When a company sees its revenues decrease substantially and its net income go negative, it is going to be hard pressed to raise debt because it will signal future deterioration in figures:

Please note how revenues decreased from $7 bil in 2022 to $4.9 on a TTM basis, while operating income moved from positive $2.5 bil to negative -183 mm on a TTM basis.

Given the shifting dynamics in the operating environment, the company chose to raise capital via mandatory convertible preferred shares:

March 6, 2024: Holders of the Depositary Shares will be entitled to a proportional fractional interest in the rights and preferences of the Preferred Stock, including conversion, dividend, liquidation and voting rights, subject to the provisions of a deposit agreement. Unless earlier converted, each share of the Preferred Stock will automatically convert on or around March 1, 2027 into between 7.6180 and 9.1400 shares of common stock of the Company, par value $0.01 per share ("Common Stock") (and, correspondingly, each Depositary Share will automatically convert into between 0.3809 and 0.4570 shares of Common Stock), subject to customary anti-dilution adjustments, determined based on the volume-weighted average price of the Common Stock over the 20 consecutive trading day period beginning on, and including, the 21st scheduled trading day prior to March 1, 2027.

This structure ensured the new securities did not affect debt ratios (they are listed in the equity line) and that the company did not bomb its common shareholders at the bottom. Issuing mandatory converts basically signals that the company is expecting a bottoming in share price in the near future, and thus does not want to raise common equity capital at depressed prices. Furthermore as we will see from the conversion arithmetic, the market has worked against the converts holders, which suffered losses as the common stock moved lower from the $131/share in March 2024.

Not your usual preferred shares

Investors are used to preferred shares as fixed income instruments that offer a high yield and are usually perpetual and sometimes cumulative. In essence traditional preferred shares are a fixed income instrument. ALB.PR.A is an equity instrument because there will be a mandatory conversion in March 2027, and an investor gets a certain compensation until that point (rate of 7.25%).

Make no mistake about it - via ALB.PR.A an investor is taking a view on the common shares and on lithium prices in essence. Because the company considered common shares to be underpriced in March 2024, it chose this mandatory converts preferred shares structure. Do not buy ALB.PR.A for the dividend, buy it only if you have a view on lithium prices and the common shares.

The conversion mechanics can be found in the prospectus and the quarterly report, but they boil down to the following:

  • If the common share price is below $109.41, you will get 0.457 common shares, which will be less than the $50 liquidation preference (109.41x0.457=50)
  • If the common share price is between $109.41 and $131.27 you will get common shares equal to $50/(ALB stock price).
  • If the common share price is above $131.27 you will get 0.3809 common shares.

As it stands the converts will result in common shares with a lower value, thus the depressed price of $38.77 versus issuance date (just multiply the common stock price now with 0.457 to get a rough estimate for converts when prices are below $109/share).

We do not believe we are going to have a 'V' shaped recovery in lithium prices, and even analysts agree with that view:

The average Wall St price target is $86.65 /share for the commons, with EPS going positive only in 2026:

Most of the uncertainty regarding earnings comes from geopolitical risk factors rather than company specific. ALB has cut costs and done some savvy moves with its balance sheet, all while maintaining an investment grade rating:

The above screen is the bond ladder for Albemarle, and we can see its senior unsecured debt trades very well, with the 2044 bonds yielding only 6.3% and having a BBB- rating. The market is not concerned about the solvency of the company, but equity investors (and converts investors) should be concerned with profitability.

Latest developments and risk factors

We mentioned geo-political factors before, and they should be in the center of investors' minds. China has subsidized in many instances industries which it considers critical, and any decisions to open or close mines from the jurisdiction have massive ripple effects:

Albemarle (NYSE:ALB) closed -11.5% in Tuesday's trading, leading a raft of sharp losses in the lithium sector, after executives at Chinese battery giant Contemporary Amperex Technology reportedly told employees to prepare to resume production at the Jianxiawo lithium mine and recall front-line workers, according to Bloomberg.

Kindly note the above action comes after the company was forced by the central government in August 2025 to shut production due to an expiring permit:

The halt at the Jianxiawo lepidolite mine in Jiangxi province’s Yichun city, a hub for China’s lithium production, came after the mine’s permit expired on August 9. CATL confirmed the closure on Monday (August 11), saying it is seeking a permit extension but offering no timeline for resuming output. The shutdown will last at least three months, according to people familiar with the matter cited by Bloomberg.

Make no mistake about it - Chinese producers are tightly controlled by the central government via permitting, and they represent just actors in a larger geo-political game.

In the U.S. the government is also helping with local production via loans, but the current administration is keen to also obtain an equity stake:

Lithium Americas stock doubled Wednesday as the Trump administration is seeking an equity stake in the mining company, which is based in Vancouver, British Columbia. The White House proposed the equity stake as Lithium Americas renegotiates the terms of a $2.2 billion loan from the Department of Energy for its Thacker Pass mine in Nevada, a Trump administration official told CNBC. Reuters first reported the equity stake proposal.

While from a technical standpoint lithium prices seem to have bottomed, they are nonetheless very much subject to political jousting.

Conclusion

The Series A Mandatory Convertible Preferred Shares are securities from the lithium producer ALB. Unlike traditional preferred shares, ALB.PR.A will be forced to convert into common shares in March 2027, thus representing a take on common equity. Despite the 7.25% coupon paid until then, prospective investors should look at the risk and rewards associated with the common shares, and understand the various geo-political factors at play given the massive influence on prices from Chinese producers.

data.title: Virtus AlphaSimplex Managed Futures Strategy Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The second quarter of 2025 was marked by extremely high volatility. April started with President Trump’s “Liberation Day” on which he announcement of broad-based global tariffs. These levies were far higher than anticipated, leading equity markets to pull back dramatically. Over the course of the quarter, the administration began negotiating with other countries to moderate some tariffs and pause others for 90 days; headlines about this process led to swings in assorted global markets. Slowly but surely, most markets began to recover. The S&P 500® Index, for example, hit a nadir in early April but rebounded to a new record high by the end of June.

There was some dispersion within the U.S. as larger multi-national stocks (represented by the S&P 500 and Nasdaq) outperformed domestic small/mid-cap stocks and industrials (represented by the Russell 2000® Index, Dow Jones Industrial Average, and S&P Mid-Cap 400® Index). With inflation remaining close to target rates, and apprehension about how trade-related issues would impact growth, many central banks lowered interest rates. However, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) refrained from lowering rates, taking a more cautious approach, while acknowledging that tariffs may drive future inflation. This created tension with the Trump administration, which continued to pressure the Fed to lower rates.

The U.S. dollar weakened dramatically this year partly out of fear that tariffs may have a negative impact on growth. This effect on global growth has also depressed some commodity prices, particularly within industrial inputs like base metals and energies. In addition to the economic uncertainty, markets have also been rocked by global conflicts, both ongoing (Russia/Ukraine, Israel/Gaza) and new (Israel/Iran). The U.S. involvement in strikes on Iran’s nuclear capabilities initially led to a spike in oil prices, which retreated after it appeared there would be no further hostilities.

Performance

The Virtus AlphaSimplex Managed Futures Strategy Fund Class I (MUTF:ASFYX) shares returned -11.11% during the second quarter of 2025, while the Fund’s benchmark, the Credit Suisse Managed Futures Liquid Index, returned -12.08% for the quarter. It is important to note that there are material differences between the Fund and its benchmark. Another index that may be used for performance analysis is the SG Trend Index as it reflects a peer group of diversified, primarily trend-following investment managers. This benchmark returned -5.59% for the second quarter of 2025.

The Fund maintained broad diversification across trend horizons and approaches to trend following in the second quarter. Over the course of the quarter, the Fund’s losses came primarily from equities, with additional losses from fixed income and commodities. Currencies provided some small gains.

As equity markets gyrated over the quarter, the Fund’s long positions led to losses, particularly from international developed market equities. The largest losses came from the Dow Jones EURO STOXX 50 (Europe), the MSCI EAFE Index, and FTSE 100 Index (UK). Emerging market equities and U.S. equities also detracted, although the Fund had some small gains from short positions in the Russell 2000 Index.

In fixed income, losses were driven by long positions in U.S. fixed income, which detracted across the board; most international developed market bonds also detracted. The Fund had small gains from Italian bonds, while short-term rates also detracted from performance.

Commodities were more mixed, as gains from long positions in precious metals, especially gold, were outweighed by losses from base metals, energies, and agricultural commodities. Base metal losses came primarily from long positions in copper, while energy losses were driven by natural gas and Brent crude oil. In agricultural commodities, losses from long positions in coffee outweighed small gains from sugar, soymeal, and cocoa.

Currencies were also mixed, as gains from long positions in foreign currencies (short USD) slightly outweighed losses from short positions in foreign currencies (long USD). The largest gains came from the British pound, euro, and Polish zloty, while the largest losses came from the Canadian dollar, New Zealand dollar, and South African rand.

At the model level, all three approaches detracted from performance, with the adaptive models underperforming the short-horizon and basic multi-trend approaches.

While higher short-term rates are continuing to positively impact Fund performance, markets expectations are increasing for lower rates in the second half of 2025.

Risk Management

The portfolio is managed to an annualized volatility target of 17% or less.1 We scale portfolio positions as often as daily in an effort to keep total portfolio risk within its target range. As market volatility increases, position sizes are reduced, and as market volatility decreases, position sizes are increased. The Fund’s realized volatility during the quarter was 15.2%, higher than the long-term realized volatility for the Fund. We continue to scale the size of the Fund’s positions to keep total portfolio risk at or below its target.

Outlook

Clarity remains in short supply for most markets. Despite strengthening equity markets, concerns about the long-term effect of tariffs persist, especially when the administration’s pause expires. Another major question is whether the Fed will begin to cut rates, or whether it will maintain higher rates despite pressure from the administration and lower core inflation. It remains uncertain what impact the early-July tax bill will have on market sentiment going forward.

Finally, the expansion of the U.S. involvement in geopolitical conflicts may change the calculus of these ongoing struggles and further impact global markets. In general, the hope is for smoother sailing going into the second half of 2025 than we experienced during the first half.

1The Fund seeks to target a relatively stable level of annualized volatility (as measured by standard deviation), but the targeted volatility is subject to change. There is no guarantee that the target will be achieved and the realized volatility level of the Fund can be higher or lower than its target volatility at any given point in time. Volatility is not an indicator of expected return or a measure of protection against loss.

data.title: Weekly Economic Pulse: A Goldilocks Rate Cut
data.description:
The Federal Reserve cut their target for the federal funds rate by 25 basis points last week due to concerns about labor market weakness. Job creation has certainly been weak the last 3 or 4 months but that is largely due to the uncertainty created by the Trump administration’s haphazard implementation of their economic program. Well, not entirely. It is also due to the economic program itself, if you include immigration in that category and I certainly do. Contrary to the administration’s claims, immigration is a net positive for the economy and there is no evidence that immigrants take jobs from American citizens. There may be reasons to limit immigration but economic growth is not one of them. Growth is a function of workforce growth and productivity growth. The Trump economic bet has always been that they can get enough of the latter to offset their stifling of the former. Unfortunately, kicking immigrants out is quick and investing to raise productivity is not. So, the economy has slowed and the Fed, like everyone else, sees this in the economic data, especially the employment data.

The quandary facing the Fed is that we don’t know how much these policy changes will reduce growth and for how long. We also don’t know their impact on prices. Targeted tariffs, such as those used by Trump in his first term, cause relative price changes but the impact of universal tariffs is more difficult to predict, especially when combined with reduced immigration. If the Fed cuts rates due to concerns about growth they could actually create the inflation they fear. If lower immigration has reduced the economy’s capacity for growth – and it certainly has – and the Fed tries to maintain the pre-tariff rate of NGDP growth, it seems likely that the result will be higher inflation. Trying to maintain the same level of growth when the economy’s potential has been reduced would likely result in labor shortages, higher wages, and higher inflation.

Did the Fed do enough last week to avoid recession? Did they do too much so that we’ll get another burst of inflation? Based on the market reaction so far, it seems they got it just about right. Long-term nominal and real interest rates are up modestly since the cut, indicating a small rise in growth expectations. The 10-year Treasury yield is up about 12 basis points and the 10-year TIPS yield is up about the same; inflation expectations are unchanged since the cut. The economy has been growing a little below the long-term trend this year and the market seems to indicate that the Fed’s move will keep it that way.

Environment

The dollar and interest rates remain in short and intermediate-term downtrends but we may be setting up for a bounce in both. The dollar’s descent has stalled, essentially unchanged since mid-June. We made a new closing low last week but there was no follow through after the rate cut. Given current market positioning and sentiment, a bounce in the buck seems likely. We had an upside probe in late July but it failed quickly. Will this one face the same fate? I don’t know but with basically everyone negative on the dollar, a quick, strong rally seems more likely than not.

A rally in the dollar would probably coincide with a rise in US interest rates but I don’t think this is like last year’s September rate cut which pushed rates up 120 basis points over the last quarter of the year. The economy is facing more headwinds now and I think investors will need convincing before they bet on a return to trend or above trend growth.

Economy/Market Indicators

The spike in credit spreads we saw after the reciprocal tariff announcement in early April has been almost entirely erased. The low for this cycle was 2.59% so we’re just 10 basis points away. If the market gets worried about recession, widening spreads will likely be our first warning sign.

Economy/Economic Data

  • The Chicago Fed National Activity index improved from -0.28 to -0.12. The 3 month average improved from -0.20 to -0.18. All the sub-indexes (production & income, sales/orders/inventories, employment and personal consumption/housing) are right around the zero level indicating trend growth. We use the 3 month average to smooth out the month to month movements and a reading of -0.18 means growth slightly below trend. In fewer words – nothing has changed.
  • The Philly Fed manufacturing survey, unlike last week’s Empire State version, rose strongly. Why Philly is rising and NY isn’t I don’t know.
  • The Richmond Fed survey looked more like the NY version last week, dropping from -7 to -17
  • Jobless claims fell back to 231k after last week’s Texas weirdness. Companies may not be hiring much but they aren’t firing many either.
  • Housing starts and permits were down hard and improvement will only come with an improvement in sales; inventories are getting a little high.
  • The inflation data from Europe was generally pretty good, although UK is still quite high at 3.7% year over year. However, the rate of change is slowing and the MPC (UK equivalent of the FOMC) voted to hold rates steady.

data.title: Copper Jumps After Grasberg Force Majeure
data.description:
By Ewa Manthey and Warren Patterson

Metals – Copper jumps after Grasberg force majeure

LME copper (HG1:COM) surged 3.6% yesterday to settle above $10,336/t (the highest since May 2024), after Freeport-McMoRan declared force majeure, meaning it would be unable to fulfill contracts to customers from its giant copper mine in Indonesia due to forces beyond its control.

Operations at Freeport’s Grasberg mine, which is the world’s second-largest copper mine, were halted nearly three weeks ago after a fatal mudslide in which at least two people died.

The mine accounted for around 3% of global copper supply this year (prior to the disruptions), nearly 30% of Freeport’s copper output and 70% of its gold production, underscoring the scale of the impact.

Freeport said it was not possible to forecast future copper and gold production from the mine and that the disaster was “likely to result in the deferral of significant production in the near-term”. It said a return to previous operational levels “could potentially be achieved” in 2027.

A prolonged disruption at the Grasberg mine could drive copper prices even higher, while intensifying supply challenges for smelters already facing feedstock shortages.

It adds to disruption in global copper supplies. Earlier this week, Hudbay Minerals announced a shutdown at its Constancia mill in Peru due to ongoing political protests.

Energy – EIA reports oil inventory draws

Oil prices edged higher yesterday, with ICE Brent rising 2.5% (the biggest intraday gain since July) and settling above $69/bbl. The upward rally was supported by persistent risk to Russian oil supply, along with the weekly storage report from the US, which showed a larger-than-expected drop in US crude oil inventories. Recent reports suggest that two major oil ports on Russia’s Black Sea coast halted the loading of tankers following overnight warnings of drone attacks. The latest threats of attack underscore the severity of Ukraine’s constant disruptions to Russia's energy infrastructure. It is estimated that the Sheskharis and CPC facility exports well over 2m b/d of Russian and Kazakh oil to global markets, holding a major share of the world’s petroleum supply chain.

US weekly inventory numbers from the EIA yesterday were also somewhat bullish for the oil market. US commercial crude oil inventories reported declines of 607k barrels for a second consecutive week, in contrast to the market expecting a build of 232k barrels. Total oil stocks stood at a little over 414m barrels, 4% below the five-year average and are now at the lowest since January. However, the decline was lower when compared to the 3.8m barrel draw the API reported the previous day. Crude oil exports eased from their two-year peak but still stand above 4m b/d, while imports rose by 803k b/d to 6.5m b/d over the reporting week. Meanwhile, crude stocks at Cushing rose by 177k barrels after declining for two consecutive weeks to 23.7m barrels over the reporting week.

For refined products, gasoline stocks fell by more than 1m barrels and are at the lowest level since late November, while demand rose for the second straight week. This should support already wide gasoline refining margins, which are unusually strong for this time of year. Similarly, distillate fuel stocks fell by 1.7m barrels, against expectations of a 468k barrel increase.

Agriculture – China diversifying agriculture import sources

Recent reports suggest that China has ramped up its soybean purchases from Argentina this week, increasing shipments from 20 cargoes earlier to 35 cargoes, with most scheduled to load in November. Some cargoes have also been booked for shipment next year from Argentina’s new crop. This surge follows Argentina’s decision to cut soybean export tariffs from 33% to 26% last month and reflects China’s broader shift away from US agricultural sourcing. Meanwhile, in the first weeks of the US marketing season, China hasn’t booked a single American cargo (the first in USDA records dating back to 1999). China’s soybean imports from Argentina increased 87% month-on-month to 1.05mt last month, the highest since October 2024. If all booked shipments are delivered, the total volume will exceed 2.27mt, surpassing Argentina’s monthly record of 2.23mt set in July 2015.

data.title: ONEOK: Highest Yield In Years, With Growth And Upside Ahead
data.description:
Investment thesis

ONEOK (NYSE:OKE) offers its highest yield in the last two years at a time when the company expects to continue growing at mid to upper single digits rates.

I expect that next year the dividend will be increased to $4.28 per share. Assuming a 5% yield, this gives a fair value of $85 per share.

Background

ONEOK is one of the largest midstream operators in the US with about 60,000 mile pipeline network.

The company

y is regionally diversified across the main basins. While it was originally focused on natural gas, its 2023 acquisition of Magellan Midstream Partners significantly diversified the company into crude oil and refined products pipelines, making ONEOK one of the largest diversified midstream companies in North America. Almost one third of its EBITDA comes from oil related assets, one third from Natural Gas Liquids and the remaining third from Natural Gas.

Over the last couple of years the company has been very active in the M&A market and in 2024 it bought Medallion Midstream and a controlling interest in EnLink Midstream for $5.9 billion. This transaction was done at an attractive valuation of 6.3x estimated 2025 EBITDA, including synergies, which are now being realized.

Moreover, in 2025 ONEOK continued its M&A and acquired the remaining interest in the Delaware basin JV for $940 million.

The company not only buys assets, in 2024 the company also sold three interstate gas pipelines to DT Midstream for $1.2 billion at a great valuation of 10.8x EBITDA.

This diversification allows the company to continue to grow in the future. I find the LPG export terminal particularly interesting, and it is expected to be completed by early 2028. All this growth will ensure that the company is capable of continuing to increase its dividend, which is the main way that the company rewards its shareholders.

The company aims to increase its dividend at an annual rate between 3% and 4% and has a long history of increasing it. Even during COVID, the company maintained its dividend. At the current stock price, this represents almost a 6% yield, the highest in the last two years.

The company not only returns capital to shareholders through dividends, it also has a $2 billion repurchase authorization; however, in 2025 it has only repurchased 190,000 shares in Q1 for $17.5 million. In 2024 it repurchased 1.675 million shares for $172 million.

What allows such constant increases and predictability is that around 90% of ONEOK’s business is fee based for every segment. For example, this is the Natural Gas Liquids segment:

Financial position and Valuation

ONEOK has a comfortable financial position. At the end of Q2 the company had just $97 million in cash while it had $1.6 billion in short term debt and $29.6 billion in long term debt. However, after quarter end, the company issued $3 billion in notes, which will be used to repay all its outstanding $1.2 billion commercial paper and the September notes.

I expect that the company will use the remaining proceeds to pay off the January and March bonds. However, if financial conditions continue to improve, I expect that the company will seize the opportunity and issue a new bond in January to deal with the $750 million maturing in November 2026 and possibly the $1.75 billion maturing in 2027.

The company target is to reduce debt to 3.5x EBITDA. Right now, it stands at 3.8x; however, it could reach its target next year with the expected EBITDA growth.

The company reduced its 2026 EBITDA expectations due to softer oil prices; however, it still expects mid to upper single digits growth, which will bring EBITDA above $8.6 billion. Formal guidance is expected by February 2026.

As a midstream operator, ONEOK valuation is highly linked to its dividend as well as its possible growth and sustainability.

The company intends to increase its dividend by at least 3% annually while maintaining a payout ratio of 85% or lower. Given the expected EBITDA increase and current payout below 80%, I expect that the company won’t have any problem increasing its dividend over the next few years. I expect that next year dividend will increase to $4.24 for almost a 6% yield.

In the last couple of years its dividend yield has been considerably lower, and I expect that the stock will rise to return to its previous yield levels. Assuming a 5% yield the stock fair value will be $85 per share, representing a 15% upside.

At $85 per share, the company enterprise value will be $85 billion which represents a 10x EV/EBTIDA on 2026 expected EBITDA mentioned earlier. A ratio slightly below its 5 years average that sits at 11.25x which means this is still a conservative fair value.

Risks

As mentioned before, most of ONEOK business is fee based which isolates the company of short term fluctuations; however, 10% of its business is commodity linked. In fact, due to reductions in commodity prices, the company revised downward its 2026 EBITDA expectation by 2%. If this situation worsens, there could be more negative revisions.

Another risk is the realization of synergies. The company has already realized several synergies on its acquisitions; however, management has to demonstrate it can accomplish all the synergies announced.

Finally, another risk is tax loss selling. The stock is down 27% this year and could be a candidate for tax loss selling.

Conclusion

ONEOK’s business is performing as expected, and mid to upper single digit growth is expected for next year. This will ensure the dividend increase which is the main driver of the stock.

The current dividend yield is the highest it has been in the last couple of years and I expect this situation to revert given the expected growth and modest payout ratio.

Assuming a 5% yield, the stock should be valued at $85 per share, representing a 15% upside. This valuation is conservative, and I wouldn’t be surprised to see the stock near $95 per share.

Market Review

Intense negative sentiment toward risk assets remained anchored in place as the calendar transitioned to the second quarter of 2025. The negativity was evidenced by a precipitous price decline for the S&P 500® Index (SPX) in the early days of April. A far more aggressive approach to global trade introduced on April 2 by President Trump’s administration furthered the negativity. The SPX began the second quarter trading at 5611.85, and by Monday, April 7, had declined to an intraday low of 4835.04.

However, patience proved to be the correct approach during the early April decline as corporate earnings, one of the primary tailwinds for the secular bull market since the fall of 2022, emerged again to support equity prices. SPX earnings growth of 12.72% continued a two-year streak of positive SPX earnings growth and profit margin expansion. This fostered a remarkable price recovery that resulted in the SPX closing the quarter at a record high of 6204.95.

The stunning market resiliency defied concerns surrounding elevated geopolitical tensions between Israel, the U.S., and Iran. Those tensions led to increased volatility for the price of WTI Crude Oil, with a wider-than-average quarterly trading range of $55.12 to $78.40. Supporting the quarter’s overall bullish resiliency, by June 30, crude oil spot prices had retreated to $65.11, continuing to provide a modest tailwind for consumer spending and the overall economy.

The Federal Reserve maintained its interest rate policy during the quarter. However, the Treasury market demonstrated persistent volatility during the quarter, with a range of 3.856% to 4.624%. Once again, patience was the right strategy. Despite the elevated volatility, Treasury yields closed the quarter relatively unchanged from the first quarter. On March 31, the 10-year U.S. Treasury closed at 4.205%, and on June 30, it closed at 4.228%.

Global equity markets continued their year-to-date outperformance, relative to the U.S., represented by outperformance in Germany, Brazil, Mexico, Spain, Canada, China, and the U.K. The positive investment thesis surrounding artificial intelligence (AI) innovation and cybersecurity reignited the bullish momentum for semiconductor and technology stocks. The factors of growth and momentum also enjoyed renewed bullish momentum and outperformance, relative to their factor peers.

How The Fund Performed

Performance was in line with the Terranova U.S. Quality Momentum Index throughout the quarter, with nominal tracking error. For the quarter, JOET posted a total return of 11.68% at NAV versus the Index’s return of 11.87%. For comparative purposes, the S&P 500 Index returned 10.94%. For the one-year period, JOET posted a total return of 19.93% at NAV versus the Index’s return of 20.43%. For comparative purposes, the S&P 500 Index returned 15.16% over the same period.

Sector Allocation Effect

  • Sector allocation was a detractor from performance over the quarter, relative to the broader market, as measured by the S&P 500 Index.
  • Top absolute contributors were the information technology, industrials, financials, and communication services sectors. The worst sectors in terms of absolute performance were the energy, consumer staples, real estate, materials, and consumer discretionary sectors.
  • Relative to the S&P 500 Index, positive contributors from sector allocation came from the health care, financials, utilities, and materials sectors. The largest detractors were the information technology, consumer discretionary, consumer services, energy, and consumer staples sectors.

Stock Attribution

  • Stock selection contributed to performance over the quarter, relative to the broader market, as measured by the S&P 500 Index.
  • The largest individual contributors to performance were Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), Vistra Corp. (VST), Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), Oracle Corp. (ORCL), and Axon Enterprise Inc (AXON). The largest detractors were Yum China Holdings, Inc. (YUMC), Diamondback Energy, Inc. (FANG), EOG Resources, Inc. (EOG), Brown & Brown, Inc. (BRO), and Baker Hughes Co (BKR).

data.title: Home Depot's Tailwinds Fully Priced In - Potential Correction Ahead
data.description:
Prior HD Coverage

I previously covered The Home Depot, Inc. (NYSE:HD) in October 2024, discussing how its intermediate-term prospects appeared brighter, thanks to the Fed's rate cut, the potential boom in real estate sales, and the boost in home improvement projects.

Despite this, I believed that the stock's prior rally had been overdone, with the expensive stock valuations and prices warranting a deep pullback for an improved margin of safety, resulting in my reiterated Hold rating then.

In this article, I shall be covering why HD remains a hold despite the promising macro/industry-wide developments and the positive comparable sales performance, attributed to the stock's premium valuations, the pulled-forward upside potential, and the reversing technical indicators implying a near-term correction.

HD's Rally Occurred Overly Fast & Furious Despite Macro Tailwinds

HD 1Y Stock Price

Since then, HD has had an exciting year with the stock pulling back as expected to my prior price target ranges of $340s during the worst of the April 2025 meltdown, before valiantly climbing back to the $410s ranges by the time of writing.

Part of its tailwinds may be attributed to the increased home sales activities as reported by numerous home builders and iBuying companies in the recent Q2'25 earning season, with it signaling the growing consumer confidence despite the challenging macroeconomic environment.

This is significantly aided by the moderating 30Y Fixed Rate Mortgage Averages to 6.26% as of September 25, 2025 and the 15Y rate at 5.41%, thanks to the recent Fed rate cut, down from the recent peak levels of 7.04%/ 6.27% albeit still a distance away from the 2019 averages of 4.13%/ 3.28%, respectively.

While the magic mortgage rates of 5% have yet to materialize, it is apparent that the market may already be on the path to macroeconomic normalization, especially since the Fed has signaled more rate cuts before the end of 2025, which are likely to trigger robust home sales and improvement activities from Q4'25 onwards.

These are important metrics indeed, since they have partly contributed to HD's robust comparable sales growth by +1.4% YoY in the US in FQ2'25, compared to the +0.2% YoY reported in FQ1'25 and -3.6% YoY in FQ2'24, especially since July 2025 boasted further recovery to +3.3% YoY.

HD has been able to outperform its direct competitor as well, Lowe's (NYSE:LOW), at +1.1% YoY, -1.7% YoY, -5.1% YoY, and +4.7% YoY over the same time period, respectively, with it underscoring why the former has been able to trump the latter over the past year, in terms of stock price recovery.

Much of HD's tailwinds are attributed to the positive comparable sales for both the Pro and DIY segments, with it exemplifying how the management's strategic push into the Complex Pro segment has been extremely successful as their DIY demands also grow during the summer season.

This is on top of the ongoing digital platform push, which resulted in +12% YoY sales growth compared to a year ago at +4% YoY, thanks to the expanded capabilities for both same-day and next-day delivery services, which in turn contributed to "a double-digit lift in spend with customers who utilize for faster delivery options as they return more frequently to shop in stores and online" for both Pro and DIY segments.

These reasons may also be why HD has been able to report a +2.6% YoY growth in big ticket comp transactions of over $1,000, compared to a year ago at -5.8% YoY, despite the "softer engagement in larger discretionary projects."

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why HD has felt confident to reiterate their FY2025 guidance with comparable sales growth of +1% YoY and adj EPS performance at $14.93 (-2% YoY), with it being well supported by the H1'25 performance at +0.4% YoY and $8.03 (-2.4% YoY), respectively.

This is especially since the promising macro developments are likely to trigger a sequentially robust H2'25 performance, thanks to the higher "smaller home improvement projects," as hinted by the management where FQ2'25 "performance across the business was the strongest we've seen in over 2 years."

As a result, I can understand why the consensus forward estimates remain promising, with HD expected to report an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +4.5%/ +9.1% between the management's FY2025 guidance to the consensus FY2027 estimates, respectively.

This is compared to the 5Y historical growth profile at +7.7%/ +7.8% while nearing the 10Y mean of +6.7%/ +12.5%, respectively, with it underscoring the company's likely to be rapid financial recovery as the macroeconomic outlook improves.

HD's Premium Valuations Trigger Risks To Its Investment Thesis

HD Valuations

These promising macro reasons may also be why the market had upgraded HD's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 27.66x, compared to the 1Y mean of 25.28x, the 5Y mean of 22.38x, and the 10Y mean of 21.36x.

Even so, I believe that the baked-in optimism may have been prematurely done, attributed to a notable premium compared to its direct competitor, LOW at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 21.14x, compared to the 1Y mean of 19.96x, the 5Y mean of 17.61x, and the 10Y mean of 17.66x.

This is especially since HD also boasts a relatively expensive estimated FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 3.03x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 27.66x and the consensus 2Y adj EPS growth estimates at a CAGR of +9.1%, compared to the 5Y mean of 3.67x and the 10Y mean of 1.70x.

A similar conclusion may also be observed after comparing to LOW at 2.48x, compared to the 5Y mean of 4.08x and the 10Y mean of 1.09x, with it underscoring why HD's recent rally may have occurred overly fast and furious.

This is especially since HD's net debt levels are increasingly bloated with an estimated net-debt-to-EBITDA-ratio of 2.11x over the LTM compared to FY2019 levels of 1.83x, albeit still improved against LOW at 2.50x and 2.36x, respectively.

This is worsened by HD's lower inventory turns at 4.6x compared to a year ago at 4.9x, with it implying overstocking and/ or softer demand at a time of uncertain macroeconomic environment.

As a result, I urge readers to monitor HD's execution closely over the next few quarters.

HD Stock Is A Hold After All, With Recent Momentum Implying Near-Term Correction

HD 5Y Stock Price

At the same time, HD has notably failed to break out of its all-time resistance levels of $439s range while seemingly reversing in momentum by the time of writing, with it apparent that the premium valuations have been overdone.

HD's Technical Indicators

A similar peaking has been observed in HD's technical indicators as well, with a further correction likely from current heights, as similarly observed at the end of 2024.

This conjecture is not overly bearish as well, since it is apparent that the stock remains expensive at my base-case fair-value estimates of $334.10, based on the management's FY2025 adj EPS guidance at $14.93 (-2% YoY) and the 5Y mean of 22.38x.

Based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $17.77, there remains a minimal margin of safety to my base-case long-term price target of $397.60, with it apparent that HD's recent recovery by +22.5% since the April 2025 bottom has been overly done.

While there appears to be an excellent upside potential of +19.6% to my bull-case long-term price target of $491.50, it is highly contingent on the stock sustaining its rather expensive FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 27.66x - a speculative notion, given the premium compared to its historical trends, peer, and sector median.

Combined with the stock's inability to break out from recent heights, the premium valuations, and the pulled forward upside potential, I prefer to maintain my Hold rating for the HD stock here.

Counter Argument

There are risks that my bearish HD thesis may not develop well, since the positive July 2025 comparable sales may very well flow into August/September 2025, with it potentially triggering an outsized FQ3'25 performance and further breakout post earnings call some time in November 2025.

These reasons are also why interested investors may want to observe the stock price movement for a little longer and add upon a deep retracement to the established 2024/2025 floor of $330s for a lower dollar cost average, with those levels also nearer to my base-case fair value estimates.

Given the -19.6% downside from current levels, I believe that some patience may be more prudent in the meantime.

data.title: Yara International: Lowering My PT Slightly Despite Strong Results
data.description:
In my latest results, I doubled down on my positive expectations and thesis for Yara International (OTCPK:YARIY) and my 390 NOK expectation and target. Since the company peaked some years ago, dropping to below 300 NOK, I have re-entered the company as an investment at a respectable 0.8% position size. That position has mostly outperformed the broader market (SPY) since I seriously started adding again, and you can follow my positive expectations for the past 3 articles, the latest of which can be found here.

Fertilizers are something that I am very happy to invest in at the right price. Yara International is different than most of the other bigger ones, because it doesn't focus on something like potash, but rather does chemical fertilizers. It's one of the world leaders in this field of urea-based fertilizer products, also called compound fertilizers with added nutrients. The company doesn't only do this, but adds specialized products to suit customers' needs as well, including a whole suite of digital applications connected to sensors to maximize yield and efficiency of farming worldwide.

With well over 100 years of operational History, and with the backing of the Norwegian state, which is a state owner, one of the few drawbacks of this company is the current limited yield of less than 2%. There are fertilizer companies that yield much more, but there aren't many fertilizer companies that are close to as safe or as qualitative as this one.

We have 2Q25 results - and those are the ones I'll use for the update of expectations and PT for the company.

Excellent 2Q25 results and prospects for 2025E confirm the short to medium-term upside.

As I've detailed in previous articles, fertilizer companies tend to be either "feast or famine" - quite volatile businesses, and one of the challenges is to forecast the specifics of these ups and downs. In the last "peak", I divested my position a bit too late, missing out on some profits. When the company dipped significantly due to challenging macro conditions, I was ready and added, especially once the company dropped below a native 310 NOK level.

In 2025, and 2Q25 especially, we have a bit of a reversal in the cycle, seeing significantly improved profits and sales trends. Yara managed a double-digit, 27% EBITDA increase on a YoY basis, increasing both top line as well as improving operating margins, while at the same time delivering a record-high production level.

Yara has always been good at efficiency and asset management - CapEx and the like. This quarter is no different. The company is currently going through a cost reduction program, and this program is ahead of schedule.

The combined savings, better energy prices, and much better sales trends resulted in a massive improvement in adjusted earnings - a 300% improvement from the YoY period.

Yara has maximized the output and efficiency of existing assets, while it's investments into those same assets have ensured yet further reduction in GHG emissions. Since 2019, the company's emissions have been down by close to 10%.

In 2Q25, every single segment reported either improvement in EBITDA, improvement in ROIC, or improvement in both numbers - and at the same time, the company's cost reduction planning continues.

Now that we're out of the summer period, how is the order book looking? Typically, we see stability during 3Q, then slowly declining during the end of 3Q, 4Q, and 1Q, until bottoming in very early 2Q. But pricing for both NPK and nitrates has been very impressive, which is part of what has been driving the company's excellent results.

The reason for the increase in premiums for these key products is mixed. First off, disruption from the Russian invasion of Ukraine and stops for Russian trade continue to affect the global market. Energy costs continue to be high across many sectors as well - natural gas is a key feedstock for the production of nitrogen fertilizer, which has the net effect of reducing the global supply/output, and which is driving fertilizer prices.

While it may be an oversimplification to claim that this trend will continue as long as Russia is out of the world trade for fertilizer (along with Belarus, which also affects things), it's fair to call it, at the very least, a pattern. Even if the war ends, it's too early to say what it will take for Russia to be "opened back" up for trade.

However, a key lesson to learn from the last time the company peaked is not to overstate or overexpect outperformance from Yara or from any fertilizer company. The current expectation and forecast is a significant 2025E EPS increase, upwards of 110% compared to the roughly 20 NOK AEPS for 2024. FCF is likely to come in at excellent levels as well for the full year.

It is unlikely, based on current trends, for more capacity additions to come online in the near future (in terms of Urea). The current projects expected to be completed between 2026-2029E are more uncertain, and are unlikely to change the end markets in any major way. As such, I expect the coming 2-5 years to be more stable than we've seen before. That set of expectations also includes where, even if Russia is welcomed back to the global market, I believe there will be a larger amount of hesitancy due to increased protectionism and alternative sourcing already established not only for the US, but for Europe, for South America, and for Africa as well. Remember, Yara International is already active in Africa, with significant operations in breadbasket countries like Kenya, Ghana, Tanzania, Zambia, Uganda, Morocco, South Africa, and others.

In my previous article, I pointed out the expected EPS increase potential that we're starting to see materialize here. Strong volumes, which are here, and good margins, also here, are the cause for this. Add to that forward lower CapEx, a better and more efficient portfolio, and I wouldn't see Yara International to be justified at a low valuation anymore - even in the case of a longer-term fertilizer decline. I'm talking about some years back when some analysts had 200-250 NOK targets for the company.

What risks remain at this juncture are related mainly to macro and geopolitical factors that it in no way controls. We're talking about things like nitrate and NPK premiums, GHG emission costs, cyclicality in fertilizers related to things like environmental and climate trends, aside from energy costs. Energy costs is probably the biggest one here, due to the significant gap in costs in central Europe compared to other fertilizer production regions like NA and Asia.

Another thing to keep an eye on is the company's personnel reduction plans, which could further enhance profitability (though I expect changes from this to be relatively small compared to other factors).

Overall, 2Q25 was a good quarter as expected, and in line with the positive estimates I expect for the full year. In terms of valuation, it's somewhat lower than in my last article - and we have updated expectations for the periods after 2025E.

Let's look at the company valuation and targets.

Valuation for Yara - Still appealing, even if I slightly lower my target.

The primary investment hurdle is the yield, which is very low at below 1.5%. Other than that, Yara has superb BBB fundamentals and is one of the safest, if not the cheapest-producing fertilizer companies around.

When it comes to valuing the company, I use very long-term P/E ranges to ensure that we integrate both the volatility of significant ups, as well as significant downs into the averages. On a 20-year average, the company's P/E is about 14.2x P/E. To highlight how much this varies from the shorter-term, now with improved EPS, the 1-year average is over 23x P/E, and when the company peaked during one of my earlier investment periods, it was over 30x P/E. The best way I know how to offset this is to use only longer-term earnings trends, and to buy or to rotate using averages, and to especially note any significant spikes in valuation.

A 14.2x P/E average is, according to me, a good one to use to forecast. Going forward, I do not use the 2025E EPS target, which currently is at 41.5 NOK, but rather average out the 5-year trend, which comes to about 30 NOK - and also incidentally close to what is expected for the 2027E period.

As you can see, there's an expected decline over the coming 2 years. At an average EPS estimate of 30 NOK, and using the 14.2x P/E, I give the company a fair value target of about 430 NOK - that's what I believe the company can "achieve", or how high it can go. The price target is the point where I can get a 15% annualized RoR to that estimate. My last target was 390 NOK - but that would not generate the sort of returns i'd be looking for at my lower forward EPS estimate. As such, I have to lower my target - if only slightly.

At 380 NOK or below, meaning about 4-5% above the current target, the company generates 15-16% annualized RoR inclusive of the company's yield. If you give the company a fair value closer aligned to the 34 NOK EPS for 2027E, this goes up to around 18% per year - but the general idea is similar here.

It might not be the best or highest-return investment available today, but it meets my targets, and I consider it very safe. Alternatives to this company? The closest peers would be Mosaic (MOS) and Nutrien (NTR) - but both of these work with slightly different mixes than Yara does, though they also produce cheaper. At 13.5x P/E, I consider Nutrien to be too expensive, even with close to a 4% yield. Mosaic has a similar EPS forecast to Yara - a high 2025E result, then declining, but despite forecasting at a similar multiple, this company does not manage to generate a market-beating rate of return.

Based on this, I would consider Yara the most interesting fertilizer company out of the three. For the ADR, my updated PT comes to $19.15/share, making the ADR YARIY a "BUY" here as well.

Risks for the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

Primary risks are the aforementioned volatile ups and downs the sector goes through - not something that Yara can do much about. With Yara also being one of the more expensive producers out there, this is also something to keep in mind when looking at Yara as a prospective investment. Also, with Yara being in the EU, the union changing rules or costs for GHG emissions is something to consider as well.

Beyond this, I would consider risks as relatively manageable, with the following longer-term thesis.

Thesis

  • Yara is one of the best/most appealing fertilizer businesses and by far the most attractive with a combination of quality/fundamentals and upside that I see in the segment even today in September of 2025. The company combines legacy appeal with future-proofing, and I see only limited downside at any sort of conservative valuation.
  • My position is now close to 1%, up over 20%, and I would consider the company attractive here. Yara is still a "BUY" here, though every investor, of course, needs to look at their own targets, goals, and strategies. I would also always consult a finance professional before making investment decisions such as this.
  • I slightly cut my target to reflect the 2026-2027E forecast potential, a PT of 380 NOK per share for the common share. As before, the native is the preferred way to go here. 2Q25 was more or less as expected, the yield continues to be low here (but next year, based on improved earnings, things could easily double in this respect), but I see a continued annualized upside of over 15% per year. This is good enough for me to invest in.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of 380 NOK/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of September 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Stitch Fix: Turnaround Finally Arrives
data.description:
As highlighted in the past, Stitch Fix, Inc. (NASDAQ:SFIX) was on a clear path to finally turn around the business. The online personalized shopping service has struggled to reinvigorate growth since the big Covid boost. My investment thesis remains ultra-Bullish on the stock due to the deep value and the opportunity to gain market share with AI innovations.

Turning The Corner

Stitch Fix has started reporting trends indicating a turnaround is in the works. The company reported the following promising FQ4'25 results:

The online personalized shopping service only beat FQ4 revenue estimates by $4 million, but the company only guided to revenues of $298 to $303 million. Stitch Fix actually beat corporate estimates for the July quarter by ~$11 million, as analysts hiked numbers based on more positive comments and a trend of beating quarterly numbers.

Back on the FQ3'25 earnings call, the CEO made the following comment regarding Active Client trends in a further sign of the business turning the corner:

In terms of overall active clients, Q3 marked our lowest quarter of sequential declines in 3 years, and the number of active clients on recurring shipments has grown for 3 straight quarters. With regards to new clients, we've achieved 2 straight quarters of year-over-year new client growth. We continue to see new clients spend more as evidenced by 90-day LTVs, which are among the highest in 3 years.

Again, the guidance for FQ4 didn't really match the commentary. Stitch Fix has seen an improving trend in Active Clients, though the number is a trailing indicator. The company only reported a 1.9% sequential decline in Active Clients for FQ4 for the best quarter in years.

Once considering FQ4 doesn't include the 13th week like last year, Stitch Fix actually reported solid growth. The adjusted numbers exclude $21.6 million in revenues from the 13th week leading to 4.4% growth YoY from actually reporting FQ4 revenues of $311 million.

The company has spent a lot of work on making the Freestyle product work better with the traditional Fixes setting up solid growth potential ahead. As the Active Clients return to growth, Stitch Fix already has AOV growth of 3% reaching $549 in FQ4'25.

In essence, existing customers remain strong and constantly spend more. Stitch Fix reported the other promising metrics on the FQ4'25 earnings call:

  • Fix average order value grew 12% YoY, eighth consecutive quarter of growth.
  • Men's business that delivered double-digit revenue growth in Q4 and a positive full year performance.
  • Fix channel continues to perform with Q4 revenue growth that outpaced our total growth due in part to allowing clients to build a Fix around a Freestyle item.
  • 90-day new client LTVs at 3-year highs.
  • Stylist Connect seeing higher order values from clients who have been part of the early rollout.

Besides these positive trends, Stitch Fix is working on an AI style assistant to expand on the current client stylist relationships and build on generative AI technology.

Incredible Multiple Expansion

Stitch Fix guided to a return to reporting actual growth in FY26. The guidance for FQ1'26 is for growth of ~5% with revenues of $335.5 million.

These numbers are meaningful due to the forecast of the highest revenue numbers since the company reported $365 million in FQ1'24. Last October quarter, Stitch Fix only produced revenues of $319 million, down $46 million YoY versus guidance for this quarter of a boost of at least $14 million from the prior year period.

Just as important, management guided to FY26 revenues of $1.28 to $1.33 billion. Consensus estimates were forecasting a dip to only $1.23 billion, down 2.2%, for the potential to beat estimates by $100 million.

The stock initially rallied to over $6 in after-hours trading. The base case is for Stitch Fix to triple to the consumer discretionary average forward EV/S multiple of 1.34x.

Stitch Fix has the potential to trade up to the following price targets based on a cash balance of $243 million and the current revenue target of $1.3 billion:

  • 3x EV/FY26 revenues of $1.3B = $31
  • 5x EV/FY26 revenues of $1.3B = $51

If the company can return to double-digit growth rates, the stock could easily hit these targets and move on from trading like a department store stock due to the AI platform and a return of excitement on the concept similar to what sent Stitch Fix to over $100 back in early 2021. Not to mention, Stitch Fix would actually cruise past these revenue targets and FY27 could reach $1.5 billion versus the current analyst estimate of only $1.28 billion despite the company throwing out a potentially challenging macro environment.

Naturally, the big risk is for the online personalized shopping service to actually struggle to return to actual reported growth. The stock has languished under $5 for several years now and could head back to those levels again.

Stitch Fix produces positive EBITDA and cash flows and guided to FY26 adjusted EBITDA of up to $45 million. The company has a cash balance of $242 million, reducing a lot of the big risk in a scenario where growth doesn't turn positive.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Stitch Fix still isn't priced for a turnaround, trading like a department store retailer priced at a fraction of sales. The substantial opportunity is to own the stock while the market rerates the valuation based on an AI retailer with Stitch Fix reaching just 3x sales pushing the stock from $5 to $31.
